summary:
The domestication of the guinea pig (Cavia porcellus) from wild cavy ancestors occurred in the Andean highlands of western South America, in the region of modern Peru and Ecuador, around 7000–6000 BCE. Evidence from archaeological sites includes guinea pig bones that show morphological changes consistent with captive breeding, such as reduced size relative to wild cavies, indicating sustained human management of the animals.

Guinea pigs became a primary source of protein for Andean communities and remain an important food animal in the region to this day. They were also used in ritual contexts, with guinea pig remains found in offering deposits at numerous ancient Andean sites. Their domestication represents one of the few independent domestication events that took place in South America, demonstrating that the Andean highlands were an important center of human innovation during the early Holocene.
